category: Large Polyp Stony (LPS) Coral — Button / Doughnut Coral; solitary polyp; monotypic genus

nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reefs and sandy seabeds with moderate water movement; typically found resting on rubble, sand, or low-relief reef areas rather than exposed high-current reef crests

decorPreferences: at least 3 inches spacing from neighboring corals
stable substrate — avoid high-flow areas that can tumble the coral
live rock in vicinity for water quality

diet.notes: The Australian Doughnut Coral draws nutrition from two sources: symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living within its tissue that photosynthesize light into energy, and active capture of meaty food items. Supplemental target-feeding with mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped seafood, or small chunks of marine fish is broadly recommended on a weekly basis. The coral has a very large mouth and can take in surprisingly large prey items. Feeding is best done in the evening when the polyp is fully expanded, directing food onto the oral disc with a pipette or turkey baster. Any uneaten food should be removed to avoid water quality issues.

goodWith: peaceful LPS corals placed at least 15 cm away
avoidWith: other corals placed within sweeper tentacle range (~12 cm at night)
aggressive LPS corals (torch, hammer, frogspawn) without generous spacing
predatory fish that may bite or nip at the polyp (triggers, pufferfish, large wrasses)
high-flow tankmates or flow that prevents full polyp inflation

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Debra Baker (2048x1901). Tack-sharp in-situ shot of a fully inflated vivid green polyp with translucent balloon-like vesicles and a clear white central mouth (cat's-eye/doughnut form) on red coralline reef — best shows the diagnostic features.
Photo by Pierre Pericard (2048x2048). Sharp in-situ teal-green, white and orange polyp with skeletal septa clearly visible through the translucent tissue around the central mouth.
Photo by Debra Baker (2048x1536). Sharp in-situ orange/pink colour morph, dramatically inflated fleshy vesicles against a blue reef background — adds colour variety.

summary: The Australian Doughnut Coral (Cynarina lacrymalis) is a large, solitary LPS coral with a fleshy, jewel-like polyp that can reach 13 cm across. It is generally considered an intermediate-level coral, best placed on the sand bed in low flow and fed meaty foods on a weekly basis.
careNotes:
The Australian Doughnut Coral is the only species in the genus Cynarina (making it what taxonomists call a monotypic genus) and is widely regarded as one of the most visually striking large polyp stony (LPS) corals available to reef keepers. Its large, fleshy polyp fills with water and can expand to roughly twice its contracted size, displaying vivid translucent coloration in shades of green, red, pink, and brown, with rare pastel and patterned morphs commanding premium prices.

Placement: The Australian Doughnut Coral is best placed directly on the sand bed or on low-lying rubble in the lower third of the aquarium. In nature it inhabits sandy substrates and low-relief reef zones rather than high-current reef crests, so positioning it on rockwork at mid-tank height exposes it to more flow than it tolerates and is generally not advised.

Lighting: The species is generally considered to thrive under low to moderate light, in the range of 30–100 PAR. New specimens should be acclimated to tank lighting gradually, as sudden exposure to high light can cause bleaching.

Water flow: Low flow is strongly recommended. High or direct current will prevent the polyp from fully inflating and can cause tissue damage over time. If the polyp remains contracted or deflated for most of the day, reducing or redirecting flow is the first adjustment to make.

Aggression: The Australian Doughnut Coral extends sweeper tentacles up to approximately 12 cm at night to sting neighboring corals. At least 15 cm (about 6 inches) of clearance from neighboring corals is advised to prevent stinging or chemical irritation. Placement on an open sand bed typically provides natural spacing.

Feeding: While the coral receives some nutrition from the symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living within its tissue, supplemental target-feeding with meaty foods on a weekly basis is broadly recommended to maintain long-term health. The large mouth allows the coral to take surprisingly substantial prey items — whole small mysis shrimp, krill, or chopped seafood are all accepted. Feeding in the evening when the polyp is fully expanded gives the best results.

The Australian Doughnut Coral is generally considered intermediate rather than beginner-level. It requires stable water parameters and consistent target feeding but tends to be more tolerant of moderate parameter variation than small polyp stony (SPS) corals.

In modern coral taxonomy, Cynarina lacrymalis is placed in family Lobophylliidae, which was separated from the older family Mussidae based on molecular research. Some older trade sources still list it under Mussidae.

Confusion with Scolymia: The Australian Doughnut Coral is frequently confused with Scolymia corals — now reclassified as Homophyllia — in the aquarium trade. The key distinction is that Cynarina has a distinctly inflated, balloon-like polyp with translucent tissue and multiple sets of skeletal ridges (septa) visible through the tissue, while Homophyllia tends to be flatter and less inflated. Cynarina is also the sole species in its genus.

Propagation: Because the Australian Doughnut Coral consists of a single large polyp, it does not fragment (frag) well and is primarily wild-collected from the Indo-Pacific. This makes it relatively expensive — desirable color morphs can range from $150 to $400 or more — and an investment that rewards careful husbandry.
breedingNotes: The Australian Doughnut Coral is a hermaphrodite — meaning a single individual produces both eggs and sperm — and reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning, releasing gametes into the water column where fertilization occurs. Wild spawning has been documented in April. Asexual reproduction under low-light conditions has also been reported. Captive fragging is generally not feasible given the solitary single-polyp structure, and propagation for the trade is almost exclusively through wild collection.
